speaker: interviewee
text: Then I came to Kowel. Should I talk yet? Anyway, when I came to Kowel, I, uh-- I went to school. And when we finished school, that was my first-- or maybe a year and a half I went there. But when we through-- school was finished, the night after this, the Germans came into us. And they came in with such a power and such a, um-- a tremendous shock to everybody because the Russians kept on saying, that's not German planes. They're not airplanes that are coming. They didn't know. We already had a lot of Russian military there. But they really didn't-- couldn't tell the difference from their planes or the German planes that they flew through our city. And they bombed next city. And when they started bombing, they recognized that it's not theirs.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyhow, it didn't take long before they started-- the-- it took them one day to come from the border, cross Kowel. And the first few days, when they came in, they started to get adjusted in Kowel. And they grabbed everybody from the street, from-- from the houses, to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I was, here, one-- one side of the city, which I lived near the school with my cousin's in-laws. And I wanted to go to my uncle and see them. And this was on the other side of the city. When I came there, they, um-- the Germans came in. And they took us to work. Took-- they took a school to make for the soldiers' place to live. I came there. There were some women, older women, younger women. And, uh, they started-- I saw through the window. They took us inside to clean inside. The men were working outside, carrying lumber or whatever they did. They didn't just let them work. But they hit them with big pieces of wood. And I saw my cousin, uh, the older one, not the one that I went to school with-- there were two brothers. This one was already married and had, uh, four girls. And the older one must have been about 12 years old already. And he already lived in World War I. And I was really surprised the way he said that he's not worried for the Germans. They were so good when they came in World War I. They were so good. They were nice to be with him and they-- they were very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was the one first-- because we were talking about it while the Russians were there. And we knew what was going on. And everybody said what was going on, what's going to be. He said he's not worried. They hit him so bad that he fell a few times. He carried lumber, then fell a few times.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, one German came in to the-- to the inside where we worked. And he was after me. When I, um-- I-- let's say one came and told me to wash this. So this one came in and said, no, you go there. So I went there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then he comes in again and says, no, you go there. So I saw something was wrong. Why is he letting the older women do that and he just takes me there? So while I was in Kowel, a girl from Germany came running away from the ghetto. Her fiance was in Kowel. And he was living in my uncle's house. He worked with them for the Russians and was a carpenter. And she told us everything, what was going on over there. So she mentioned once, that she said, they're raping women. They're raping. But they just like virgins. If you say you're married, they don't bother with you. So I saw that he's after me with something, and I was very scared. So when I figured he's so good to me, doesn't let me scrub this and doesn't scrub for some reason. So it got to me, maybe I'll use that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to him, sir, I would like to ask you a favor. I just had a baby. And I came to my aunt and uncle to get some milk for it, and they grabbed me to work here. And I have to go home and feed the baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said, OK. Go. And I thought that he's going to-- well, because there was one way. I figure he will shoot me. If not, there was a-- a well in the yard. And I figured I'd just jump in there. So I, uh-- I asked him. And he said, go ahead. Go home. And when I walked, I figured he's going to shoot me from the back. But he didn't. He just let me go. Maybe he was just a nice person.

speaker: interviewee
text: He let me go. And I went through the gate to the school. And I still was worried that, you know, he's going behind to shoot me. Until I got to my aunt's house, I was fainting almost. I was not-- I didn't know what's going on. And since then-- and then I went back where I lived to my cousin's in-laws. And I said, no more. Now I'm not going to, uh, have anybody grab me on the street to work. I went to a Judenrat. They established right away a Yiddish Judenrat, for the Jews. I went to the Judenrat, and I asked them for work. I want to go to work. So they-- whenever they had the work, they sent me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember who were the members of the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I am not-- I wasn't born there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything about the ghetto in Kowel?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- of course. I remember everything about the ghetto. I'll tell you from the ghetto what it was. This is I lived-- I went back to my-- that was the first two days then, they came in, what I'm talking about.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went back. And they, uh-- they sent me to, uh-- to work to, uh-- There was Schutzpolizei. They gave me a-- a work schein-- Arbeitsschein in German. And the Schutzpolitzei was also not far away from us where I lived. So I worked for them. Here, bless you. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I forgot what you asked me about, who I, uh-- And you worked for the Schutzpolitzei.

speaker: interviewee
text: Worked for Schutzpolitzei.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were going to tell us about the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: That wasn't ghetto yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't the ghetto yet. They didn't make the ghetto right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So just first day, we worked for Germans all over. So I worked for the Schutzpolitzei. And they also-- the woman that I lived with, a sister-in-law of my cousin, they got work for her. Because she should get a schein also from them, from the Schutzpolitzei. So they-- she used to get the-- the laundry from them to wash, but at home, because she had small children. We worked over there in a few days. It didn't take long, maybe a week. A week or two I worked for them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they had a order to kill Jews. So they, um, went around in the city. A matter of fact, the man that I cleaned his room told me if I have a young-- young man or my family or somebody, he should come that Saturday to work. I didn't know why. He said they need somebody to work, so if I have somebody.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went over to my cousin, and I told him that he wants to come. He said, yeah. And that Saturday, we came to work. I think that he worked by chickens or something. Chickens-- they killed chickens. They had to clean them or something. And I worked with the cleaning.

speaker: interviewee
text: About 2 o'clock-- we didn't know anything. About 2 o'clock in the afternoon or 3 o'clock in the afternoon, this man that I worked for and that had laundry where I lived with a lady, he said to me that he, uh, needs the laundry because they leaving tomorrow. I didn't know why. So I said, OK, but I can't go. Oh, he said, I can't go by myself. He has to go with me to pick up the laundry. I said, go, and it was not too far, the street. While I was walking with him from the Schutzpolitzei to the place with the house that I lived, kids were running around in the street and telling that policemen-- you know what Schutzpolitzei is. It means that they supposed to, uh-- supposed to, uh-- uh-- um-- guard you, take care of you, what are Schutzpolitzei-- what it means-- schutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the children ran around. They said, Jude, Jude, that I am a Jude. Now, he knew that I am a Jude. But the fact that they were already absorbed with this, that they wanted him to know that I am a Jude-- because young kids didn't know that he knows, whatever. And we already wore the stars and yellow patches on. So they were running around saying, Jude. Anyway, I got home. He took the laundry. And he went back, and I didn't. He went back home. I found out that this afternoon that we all were at work, they took out people from the houses. And they-- they said-- the rumors were that they killed them, or they didn't know what they did with them. So I went back. And my next-door neighbor-- they took an old lady next door, and two girls, and I think they had a son-- I don't remember exactly-- they didn't take. Just the mother they took. And they said they take them to work. Why would they take a mother to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back. And I run into this man, and I asked him-- I said that it was my stepmother-- couldn't say mother because they had a different name-- that they took my stepmother while I wasn't there. Because the girls, my neighbors, they cried because they took their mother. He said, they already sent them away to work wherever they had to go. They not here already. They first took them in trucks, and they send them right away, where they had to kill them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, um, I came back. I said I couldn't do anything because they say they're not there already. They took them. So this was the first time-- and neighbors that lived out of the city-- not Jews, non-Jews-- came back to the city because some, you know, some meant well and some were happy, whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: But some, they said that they killed them. And they could show them where they have the grave. They killed them all in one grave. And I don't know-- people just didn't want to believe, or they couldn't believe, or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was one thing already, the first thing. Then it came a little later, and they-- closer to the holidays. They took out three, uh, butchers. And they hung them. And it was on a Thursday, before Shabbas. They hung them in the middle of the market, the middle of the city, for everybody to see. Because they probably caught them, uh, killing, uh, a cattle or something for Shabbas. So it was a-- it was a very, very, very bad thing. You just tried to survive. And, uh, when these Schutzpolitzei left, I went again to the Judenrat. So then again they gave me a work at the barracks from the army, regular army. So I went there.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a whole group of girls, we were. Because nobody wanted to be caught on the street, any place, you know, and do with you whatever they want. So we were a bunch of girls, and we worked at the barracks. And we worked for a time. There were a lot of good soldiers, plain soldiers. There were older people that knew-- didn't like what was going on. And there were younger people. Also there were nice people. They, um-- they were SS. I think that on every soldier, regular soldier, there must have been one SS in there. And they were very afraid. They were very afraid. I know that I didn't have any shoes to go to work, to walk to work. And under the pressure that you went to work, how scared I was, I got trouble with my legs. I couldn't walk on time, the tension or whatever. And I couldn't walk in the middle of the street. And I had to sit down because my feet were like pieces of wood that didn't go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I sat down on the street. For a while I was sitting. And my blood circulation came back. And I came there. So first, I thought maybe because my shoes are torn and I walk in the snow, don't have any shoes. So I went in. There was a shoemaker, a soldiers' shoemaker that fix shoes for the-- for the soldiers. And I asked him whether they could, uh, do something to my shoe, a few nails put in, a few nails or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they said to me, well, we don't have to fix these shoes. We have a pair of beautiful boots I could give you. And gave me a pair of beautiful boots, officer's, like women's boots, but tall ones. Gave me a pair of shoes to put on. And I thanked him, and that's it. I mean, I didn't see him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, uh-- but other ones were. I worked in the, uh-- the barracks. We had to clean the rooms for the officers. And we also had to do-- wash their laundry-- not theirs-- everybody's laundry, for the regular soldiers, for everything. So I and another girl from Lodz that lived over with my cousin-- they came from Lodz, and they took in people. So she lived the same place I lived. And we worked together, washing the clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she worked-- cleaned the room for one that was a SS, a murderer, plain murderer. And he came in for no reason-- for no reason came into the laundry room. There probably wasn't used to be a laundry room. It was probably for the Russian, where they cooked or something. It was a big, big-- like a platter, like a platter, steel plate. And on this, there were the big-- we put on the laundry to wash. And everything was made out of steel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he came. And for no reason, he started to, uh, holler at her, that she didn't do something right in her room-- his room, didn't do the basket, or she didn't do the dusting. I don't know what. He started hollering at her and grabbed her and threw her right against the corner from that steel, uh, oven.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she just fell. I thought she was dead because I didn't know what was going on. I didn't know. And he left, left her like this. Some soldiers came in. They called the paramedics. They took her into the paramedics. She had a brain concussion. So they kept her there-- I don't know-- all day. And then they-- you know, we had to go home. And, um, so they were-- they were very terrible, terrible SS men, murderers. But on the other hand, the people that-- the one that I worked for was a man that said, any time I have to come into the room, you go out. They were not supposed to be with Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's fine. And the same man, when he knew probably what was going on, he gave me the idea of Katya. He didn't call me-- my name was Peshe. He called me Katerina.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said to me, you really should not be here. You could have another name and live, you know, not as a Jew. Another time he came in-- came in and said to me, all this war is because of your Jewish relatives in America-- all because of your Jewish relatives in America. They want war. We didn't want war. I didn't pay attention. I didn't discuss. I just-- he told me I should walk out when he comes in. So when he started talking, I just walked out. But the idea at that time came to me when he said about, you know, I should be Katya, not Peshe. So this is when I got the idea when we got into the ghetto. And one day also came an order that Jews can't-- can't work anymore in the barracks mixed with German soldiers. So, uh, we couldn't work there anymore. All the-- about the time when we still worked, they came in, soldiers, older people. They were high-ranking people. They came in from Rowne. Rowne is a big city in Ukraine, where there were a lot of Jews. And they liquidated Rowne. There couldn't be-- there weren't any Jews anymore in Rowne. And those people, the older people, saved some Jewish girls in Rowne. And they placed them in farms, in big farms around where Germans were, their friends, older people. Mostly older people were managing farms. They couldn't go on the front.

speaker: interviewer
text: And those men-- they must have been in their 70s or 65 or something like this-- used to ask when we have to go back and we lose the war, will you let us change clothes? So we didn't know what to say. We were afraid even to discuss things like this with them. We didn't know who they are.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, when there came the time when they said that Jews can't be anymore working outside the ghetto, this is when they made a ghetto. So when the day came, the order to make the ghettos, they had two ghettos. One was a working ghetto. And one was a, uh, business ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all the business people had to be in the business ghetto. And all the working people had to be in the working ghetto. Now, the working ghetto was not, uh, from all Jews. They were still living in the working ghetto. They had make-- make place for some Jews coming in. But non-Jews were living there also.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was already-- they said until 6 o'clock, no Jew could be on the street. And if you find them, they're going to kill them. So I went with the whole family that were living there, that I was living with. And there were also some of my distant-- mother's cousins. They were living in the ghetto to begin with. They were living in that section where the ghetto was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I figured, and they also told me, I should come to them. So I went there. I went there because my uncle and aunt and cousins lived on the other side of the city.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't know what they were doing, which-- because my cousin, the older one that I said was beaten up that much by the Germans, he was a businessman. He was in business with leather and stuff. He was a businessman. So I figured he'll go probably there. But they decided he'll go to working ghetto. And about 5 o'clock, my younger cousin came running and took me right away, said, don't take him anything. Just come there. Probably rumors were going around also that something won't be good here. They didn't know if we would know we would know. But we didn't. Nobody knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he, um-- he took me there. And I went to the working ghetto. That's why I happened to be in the working ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the same night when the working-- they closed up the ghetto 6 o'clock. In the evening, there was already Jewish police for the Jews. So the police, uh, had-- were supposed to take everybody out on the market when it got dark. They said they're going to have a count at [NON-ENGLISH]. Everything will be OK. All they have to do is count the people, how many people there are. And the first thing, they brought him out to the market. It was in a marketplace, which was all empty-like, and all around were big buildings. And in the middle was nothing because there was a market where people come in with their-- they took out all these people in the market. And they already had their machine guns put up all around. And they-- the same time they just brought them out there, they started shooting. There was a policeman that took out his whole family. Because they did not know-- like some people say that the police's fault, it was this fault-- they did not tell them what it's going to be-- not the Jewish police, they didn't tell.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they killed all the people except my friend that worked with me. She was hiding in an attic. She didn't care what they said. They said, come out. If we'll find you, we'll kill you.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was from Lodz. She was hiding by herself in the attic in the house. And it was in the family's-- not her family, but the family we stayed with, their sister had a house there and a restaurant. So this is where she was hiding in the attic. Now, their whole family that I stayed with-- not in the ghetto-- were hiding. They went into a basement. And the basement was outside. You had to go around from the outside. My cousin's brother-in-law, it was his family and his mother. They were in that basement. And he wanted to go and get something, maybe some food into the-- they didn't know how long they have to stay there. I don't know what. But it was late. And the Germans saw him. And he didn't want-- he didn't want his family to be killed. So he didn't even get close to the basement. He was walking away, the other side. So they killed him right away. The family was saved in the basement and so was my girlfriend in the attic.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were saved because your cousin took you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the-- I was in the working ghetto. My cousin got me out, 5 o'clock or 4 o'clock. I can't tell you exactly when. But he said, let's go. You're going to stay with us. And that's where I was. I was in the working ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did they keep the working ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they did a lot of tricks with the working ghetto. Now they said, all the Jews that are alive in the old ghetto-- in the business ghetto should come to the working ghetto, that nothing is going to happen to them anymore. They just did what they did, and nothing's going to happen. Now, everybody came here to this ghetto already. And a few weeks later, they had an order again, that who was going to have a working schein stays in this ghetto, and who not has to go back to the other ghetto. And nothing is going to happen to them, and nothing would. Now, people got, you know, scared. Because they knew already what happened in that ghetto. By that time, I worked already at the depot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Aha.
